SUMMARY INFORMATION.Application Title: Developing statistical methodology for coenrolment in platform designs

Lead Applicant.Department: Warwick Clinical Trials Unit Professional Background Not a Health or Care Practitioner Detailed Professional Statistician Background Professional Registration

APPLICATION DETAILS.Scientific Abstract:
Background
Coenrolment—the enrolment of participants in more than one interventional study—creates efficiencies
in recruitment and trial delivery. It reduces competition between trials at the same research site
competing for similar patient populations. This approach also enables simultaneous evaluation of
multiple interventions throughout a treatment pathway. Coenrolment can improve evidence synthesis,
accelerate policy translation, and offers to expand patient opportunities to participate in and benefit from
clinical research. Despite its growing adoption in clinical trial research, there is a demand for updated,
principled methodology.
Existing guidance predates contemporary trial designs, leaving unresolved challenges around
interaction effects, bias, type I error control, and statistical power. Without robust frameworks, the
benefits of coenrolment cannot be fully validated, limiting its credibility and implementation.
Aims and Objectives
The overarching aim is to develop statistical methodology for the design and analysis of clinical trials
incorporating coenrolment. Specific objectives are i) to identify and characterise existing design and
analytical approaches for coenrolment and analogous settings, ii) to review of current statistical
practices of UK trial statisticians on trials permitting coenrolment, iii) to evaluate design and analysis
methods by studying simulations of variations of coenrolment parameters, and iv) applied validation: to
test and assess the developed methodology using real-world data from the CoReCCT platform, ensuring
practical relevance.
Methods
WP1: Scoping Review – A systematic mapping of statistical methods relevant to coenrolment. This will
produce a taxonomy of design types and analytical approaches.
WP2: Survey of Statisticians – A structured survey targeting UK trial statisticians to capture perspectives
and current statistical practices in the design and analysis of coenrolment. Ethical approval will be
obtained, and pilot testing will ensure clarity and validity.
WP3: Simulation Study – An investigation of coenrolment scenarios under varying design parameters
(e.g., treatment combinations, interaction effects, outcome measure). I will explore both trial design and
analysis models. Performance metrics will include bias, precision, type I error, and power.
WP4: Applied Analysis – Application of best-performing methods of WP3 to CoReCCT study data,
generating estimates of treatment and interaction effects and comparing results with original analyses.
Dissemination and Impact
Findings from each work package will be disseminated through at least four open-access publications
and presented at international conferences. Simulation code will be published and potentially developed
into R packages for routine use. Methodological guidance derived from this work will inform NIHR-
funded trials and shared across international research networks. Collaborative projects will be initiated
and designed during a research placement abroad.
Research Inclusion
Patient and public involvement (PPI) is integral to this project. Previous focus groups and surveys
indicate strong patient support for coenrolment, reinforcing its alignment with public preferences for
broader treatment access and research participation. A dedicated advisory board will be regularly
updated and consulted throughout the duration of the project and additional work will be undertaken to
co-produce lay summaries with members of the public.
APPLICATION DETAILS.Plain English Summary of Research:
Background
Coenrolment is when a patient enrols and participates in more than one clinical trial study.
This gives patients more opportunities to take part in research and access new treatment options for
better health outcomes. Researchers also benefit from coenrolment as it helps trials improve their
recruitment and research efficiency. Researchers can share their data with each other to study new
treatment combinations which can lead to new insights into treatment plans and future research.
Coenrolment is widely accepted among the scientific community and believe that coenrolment should be
considered more routinely when designing a clinical trial. Despite this support, the available data on
coenrolment is still limited and there is currently no clear statistical method to fully understand the results
of clinical trials that have allowed for coenrolment. More research is also needed with members of the
public, especially those involved in the consent process such as parents, family members, and other
substitute decision-makers.
Research Aim
To develop statistical methods to analyse data from coenrolment trial designs and improve its
understanding and accessibility across clinical trial research.
Project Plan
I will carry out four studies to achieve my research aim.
Study 1: I will review the research literature on statistical methods for analysing coenrolment in clinical
trial research and understand all the key factors to consider when analysing coenrolment data. I will also
look at other statistical models that can work for analysing patient data with more than one intervention.
Study 2: I will carry out a survey on UK trial statisticians to understand the current practice for handling
coenrolment in clinical trial research. I will identify knowledge gaps and areas of concern when analysing
coenrolment in clinical trials.
Study 3: I will carry out a simulation study to test different trial designs with coenrolment and their effect
on the reliability of trial results. I will also test different ways of analysing trial data with coenrolment. I will
use data from CoReCCT (the Confederation of Respiratory Critical Care Trials) – a group of 4 large
critical care trials that allow for coenrolment across their studies. I will measure and compare the
statistical performance of each trial design and analysis method.
Study 4: I will use data from the CoReCCT studies and apply the best statistical method identified in
Study 3. I will compare these results with the original CoReCCT analyses.
Dissemination & Impact
I will share findings through research papers, presentations at academic conferences, international
research placements, and public engagement events with the local community. The knowledge gained
from developing this statistical methodology can directly impact the ongoing trials in CoReCCT and
future clinical research around the world that allow for coenrolment.
Research Inclusion
An advisory board of lay members will be consulted twice per year and will undertake appropriate
training to maximise the diversity and inclusion of all involved and affected by my research. I will deliver
two workshops of 10-20 participants from the public to co-produce plain language descriptions of
coenrolment and create a glossary of terms. This can be used by researchers to update their patient-
facing material and help patients to understand coenrolment in order to provide informed consent,
reducing burden and improving accessibility.
APPLICATION DETAILS.Patient & Public Involvement:
I will form a Patient and Public Involvement (PPI) Advisory Board of 2-4 participants. I will extend my
search to wider public engagement networks within my university and through social media. There will
be no restrictions or requirement of a lived experience for this advisory board as the research has the
potential to affect patients, their family members, and substitute decision-makers. We will consult the
PPI Advisory Board in the development of the planned workshops.
Co-production of a glossary of terms of key concepts in coenrolment trial designs
A preliminary PPI focus group was consulted to explore various ways of approaching this particular
project. They recommended that a group of 10-20 participants should be consulted through in-person
and online meetings to co-produce the glossary of terms. They believed this was a sufficient number to
cover the diversity required to represent members of the public and characteristics that may affect their
comprehension of trial terminology. In-person activities are included to maximise accessibility and
inclusion of participants that may struggle with digital technologies. I will attend workshops by the PPI-
SMART group from the NIHR Leicester Biomedical Research Centre as well as consult them on their
experience in developing lay glossary of statistical terms.
Target Population
Any member of the public will be eligible to participate. Strategies will be employed to optimise inclusion
and diversity of characteristics such as age, socioeconomic status, and highest educational attainment.
Existing evidence suggests that parents and family members (as well as those often approached for the
consent) of patients are particularly underrepresented in this field of research.
Recruitment Strategies
Members of the public will be invited to participate in this research by sharing posters across notice
boards (NIHR People in Research), specialised patient communities (ICUsteps, Independent Cancer
Patients’ Voice), social media, and established networks of public contributors at WCTU. Adverts will be
posted for a maximum of 6 weeks. Public contributors will be randomly selected from the participants
that have expressed interest.
Co-production Methods
I will conduct two workshops using the nominal group technique to produce definitions. Using the results
of the scoping review and key factors highlighted by the survey of statisticians, I will produce a list of key
terms to be defined in the workshops. For each key concept or terminology, the nominal group
technique will facilitate consensus building towards a suitable plan language description of the term.
The first stage involves sending the questions in advance and letting participants reflect and record their
proposed definitions. The second stage will allow participants the opportunity to present their plain
language descriptions, as well as ask for clarifications or refine/group their ideas together. In the third
stage, the participants will be asked to rank the presented plain language descriptions, and the score will
aid in determining a consensus on which plain language descriptions are best.
Further Public Engagement
The preliminary PPI focus group was supported by the Warwick Institute of Engagement (WIE) Events
and Activities Fund. This collaboration will be continued throughout the doctoral award and we will work
together to prepare public engagement events to share research output to the local communities of the
University of Warwick.
